coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-induced construction of 0D/3D carbon quantum dots modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres: Boosting molecular oxygen activation for efficient antibiotics degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ruan, Xifang
Sun, Aiyun
Zhou, Ting
Zhang, Haijie
Ye, Meng
Zhu, Xingwang
Yi, Jianjian
Hu, Qingsong
Gu, Binxian
coredata.dc:description: Developing highly efficient heterostructured photocatalysts for contaminants removal has long been studied, which is mainly limited by ineffective capture of visible-light photon, undesirable recombination of photoinduced hole-electron pairs, and insufficient consumption of charge carriers during surface catalytic reactions. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite photocatalyst have been firstly developed through in-situ ionic liquids induced synthesis. The bridge function of ionic liquids guarantees the symmetrical disperse of CQDs around PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres, for synchronically overcoming the above drawbacks and markedly promoting antibiotics degradation efficiency: (i) CQDs modification harness solar photons from visible to near-infrared region; (ii) tight coupling of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br endows the heterojunction with enhanced separation efficiency of charge carriers; (iii) particular delocalized conjugated construction of CQDs strength the enrichment and utilization of photogenerated electrons. Benefiting from these distinguished features, the optimized CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite displays obviously enhanced photocatalytic activity towards the elimination of ciprofloxacin and tetracycline under visible light irradiation. The spin-trapping ESR spectra and radicals trapping tests demonstrate that superoxide radicals, hydroxyl radicals and holes are the major active species participated in the purification of antibiotics and the conceivable photocatalytic mechanism has been promoted. This research provides new insights into the development of CQDs modified heterostructured photocatalyst for solar energy conversion applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterojunction
Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquids
Interface engineering
Photocatalytic

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was ﬁnancially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2021M691389 ), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (NO. 22KJB610026 ), the State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse (Project NO. PCRRF21036 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Fund Project of Yangzhou University Students (NO. X20220558 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration for the formation of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite catalyst. (b) XRD patterns of the obtained catalysts. (c) Raman spectra, (d) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br. (e,f) TEM, (g) HR-TEM and (h) elemental mapping images of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br.
XPS spectra of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br: (a) Survey, (b) Pb 4f, (c) Bi 4f, (d) O 1s, (e) Cl 2p, (f) Br 3d and (g) C 1s.
Photocatalytic properties of the catalysts for the degradation of CIP (a) and TC (c) under visible light irradiation (λ＞400 nm), reaction kinetics for the degradation of CIP (b) and TC (d).

(a) UV–vis DRS of the obtained catalysts, (b) bandgaps of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br, (c) PL spectra, (d) EIS spectra, (e) transient photocurrent spectra of PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Br, PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, (f) XPS Valence-band spectra of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br.

(a) DMPO- O2 •-, (b) DMPO-•OH of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, (c) trapping experiments of active species during the degradation of CIP, (d) photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br in photocatalytic degradation.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-induced construction of 0D/3D carbon quantum dots modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres: Boosting molecular oxygen activation for efficient antibiotics degradation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, 196 West Huayang Road, Yangzhou 225127, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing highly efficient heterostructured photocatalysts for contaminants removal has long been studied, which is mainly limited by ineffective capture of visible-light photon, undesirable recombination of photoinduced hole-electron pairs, and insufficient consumption of charge carriers during surface catalytic reactions. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite photocatalyst have been firstly developed through in-situ ionic liquids induced synthesis. The bridge function of ionic liquids guarantees the symmetrical disperse of CQDs around PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres, for synchronically overcoming the above drawbacks and markedly promoting antibiotics degradation efficiency: (i) CQDs modification harness solar photons from visible to near-infrared region; (ii) tight coupling of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br endows the heterojunction with enhanced separation efficiency of charge carriers; (iii) particular delocalized conjugated construction of CQDs strength the enrichment and utilization of photogenerated electrons. Benefiting from these distinguished features, the optimized CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite displays obviously enhanced photocatalytic activity towards the elimination of ciprofloxacin and tetracycline under visible light irradiation. The spin-trapping ESR spectra and radicals trapping tests demonstrate that superoxide radicals, hydroxyl radicals and holes are the major active species participated in the purification of antibiotics and the conceivable photocatalytic mechanism has been promoted. This research provides new insights into the development of CQDs modified heterostructured photocatalyst for solar energy conversion applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis is deemed as one of the most promising techniques to alleviate environmental pollution and energy crises [1,2]. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic efficiency is still unsatisfactory. This can be attributed to the narrow absorption range of visible light, low efficiency of photocatalytic photocarrier separation and poor consumption of photoinduced carriers during the photocatalytic reactions [3,4]. Nevertheless, conventional ZnO and WO3 merely respond to ultraviolet light, which occupies approximately 4 % of the sunlight. Additionally, a variety of visible light-driven semiconductor catalysts (for example: bismuth oxybromide [5], silver halide [6], carbon nitride [7], etc.) can only be excited under light irradiation (< 500 nm). As a result, the utilization efficiency of visible light (400 nm< λ< 780 nm) is far from satisfactory. Based on the above analysis, developing catalysts with broad spectral response, high separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers and abundant catalytic sites will benefit for the enhancement of photocatalytic performance.

This decade, lead bismuth oxyhalides (PbBiO2X, X = Cl, Br, I), a kind of Sillén-structured layered catalysts, have aroused wide concern in environmental restoration and energy utilization [8,9]. These catalysts show satisfactory photocatalytic performance in the field of persistent organic pollutants removal, CO2 reduction, water splitting, and so on [10–12]. Whereas, the further development of single PbBiO2X is limited to the insufficient charge separation [13,14]. Therefore, it is tough to generate sufficient photoinduced electrons for the formation of reactive oxygen species. This results in low photocatalytic degradation performance of individual PbBiO2X. To circumvent the aforementioned problems, rational construction of PbBiO2X-based heterostructures with g-C3N4 [15], MoS2 [16], PbO [17] have been reported. In addition, hybridization among PbBiO2X is also considered to be an effective approach to optimize photocatalytic performance of PbBiO2X. This can be explained that the different band edge potentials of PbBiO2X can enable the transfer and spatial separation of holes and electrons. Although the development of PbBiO2X/PbBiO2Y (X, Y = Cl, Br, I) have not been reported until now. It can be predicted that more photogenerated electrons can be involved into the generation of reactive oxygen species, which results in the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

In the last decade, as an important member of carbon-based nanomaterial family, 0D carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have inspired extensive studies owing to their potential for a wide variety of technical applications [18]. Among the electronic and physicochemical characteristics of CQDs, their optical properties have attracted increasing interest in recent years [19,20]. More importantly, they can serve as electron transporters and acceptors thanks to their conjugated π structure [21,22]. As a result, CQDs have been applied to decorate photocatalysts, which can improve the utilization efficiency of solar light, boost the sufficient separation and transfer of photoinduced carriers, thus enhance photocatalytic performance [23–26]. In spite of this, in previous references, the core character of CQDs has not yet been studied at length. Taking into account that the diameter of CQDs is less than 10 nm, the uniform distribution of CQDs around photocatalyst need further study.

To address the aforementioned impending issues, CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure photocatalyst are prepared through ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. In this approach, ionic liquid can be employed as template and reaction source to regulate the growth of PbBiO2X crystals. More importantly, the presence of coulomb force and hydrogen bond between CQDs and ionic liquid is beneficial to in-situ anchoring more CQDs around PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br material [14,27]. And CQDs modification can boost organic contaminants adsorption, promote interfacial charge transfer and spatial charge separation, and eventually enhance photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants. This work provides a novel insight into the development of CQDs modified heterostructured catalysts for enhanced photocatalytic performance via reasonably regulating the built-in electric field at the interface.

CQDs powder is acquired on the basis of the previous literatures and then managed by lyophilization [24,25]. Citric acid monohydrate (1.06 g) was dispersed into deionized water (11 mL), and ethylenediamine (340 μL) was added into the above solution subsequently. The mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the brownish red solution was subjected to dialysis for 72 h to get CQDs solution. In the end, CQDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying for 48 h.

The typical process of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite is as follows ( Fig. 1a), CQDs (x g), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (0.48 g) and lead nitrate (0.33 g) were dissolved into ethylene glycol (18 mL). Subsequently, [C16mim]Cl (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, 0.17 g) and [C16mim]Br (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide, 0.19 g) were added into the above solution bit by bit with stirring continuously. After stirring for 1 h at room temperature, the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and reacted at 140 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sediment was collected and washed with ultrapure water and absolute ethyl alcohol for four times. The final samples were dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The loading capacity of CQDs in the nanocomposite was 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% and 8 wt%. Single PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br was synthesized without the introduction of CQDs.
For comparison, the preparation process of PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr was similar, merely replacing [C16mim]Cl and [C16mim]Br by KCl and KBr.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy is recorded on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) using monochromatic Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, 7° min−1, from 10° to 80°). The morphology was collected by Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was measured via an ESCALAB250Xi XPS (Thermo Fisher, USA) with monochromatic Mg-Kα radiation as X-ray source for excitation. A laser Raman spectrometer (DXR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was employed to collect Raman spectra with a 532 nm laser as an excitation source. A specific surface and aperture analyzer (Quadrasorb EVO, Anton Paar, USA) was used to analyze the specific surface area and pore diameter of the catalysts by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method at 77 K. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra was acquired on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was obtained by a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). An electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (JES-FA200, Bruker, Germany) was used to capture ESR signals of spin-trapped radicals using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trapping agent in water and methanol solutions. The photoelectrochemical data were obtained by a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai). A standard three-electrode cell (a catalyst/ITO as working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and a platinum wire as counter electrode) was adopted during the photoelectrochemical test. A Xenon lamp source (PLS-SXE300D/300DUV, Beijing Perfectlight) acted as the light source during the photocurrent test.

Photocatalytic oxidation degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and tetracycline (TC) over the obtained catalysts were carried out to assess their photocatalytic performance. A xenon lamp (250 W) equipped with optical filter (λ﹥400 nm) was employed as the optical source. The reaction temperature was kept at 25 °C via a recirculating cooling water system. 0.05 g of catalyst and 100 mL of CIP solution (10 mg/L) or TC solution (20 mg/L) were added into a reaction flask and stirred for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the optical source was switched on, and 4.5 mL suspensions were sampled at set intervals. The acquired suspensions were centrifuged at 15,000 r/min to acquire clear solutions. The strength of CIP and TC solutions were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450) according to their absorbances at 276 and 357 nm. To distinguish the major reactive species participated in photocatalytic reactions, various radical scavengers were added. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) can capture hydroxyl radical (•OH), ammonium oxalate (AO) and triethanolamine (TEA) can capture holes (h+), and nitrogen (N2) can inhibit the generation of superoxide radical (O2 •-).

The crystalline structures, chemical spectra and textural properties of the acquired catalysts were recorded via XRD, Raman and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. As displayed in Fig. 1b and Fig. S1, All the diffraction peaks match well with orthorhombic PbBiO2Cl (JCPDS card No. 13-0352) and tetragonal PbBiO2Br (JCPDS card No. 38-1008) [8,13]. And the diffraction peaks are intense and sharp, demonstrating the highly crystalline nature. There are no obvious characteristic peaks of impurities, demonstrating the high purity of the obtained materials. It should be noted that a broad peak centered around 25.5° can be found for single CQDs. Nevertheless, no diffraction peaks located at 25.5° can be observed for CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite. This can be attributable to the low loading amount and high dispersity of CQDs in the nanocomposite [28].

Raman spectra was conducted to confirm the existence of CQDs in CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1c, the strong peak (149 cm−1) is ascribed to the “lattice vibration” of PbBiO2X. Compared with PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br shows two obvious peaks at 1389 and 1580 cm−1, which represent the disordered (D) band and graphitic (G) band of graphene. The peak intensity of G band is much higher than that of D band, which is in agreement with previous studies [24]. There is background interference in the nanocomposite because of the strong fluorescence for CQDs. To investigate the textural properties of the as-prepared catalysts, the BET surface area measurement was carried out. In Fig. 1d, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms belong to a type IV isotherm, indicating the characteristics of mesoporous [29]. This is in accord with the pore diameter distribution analysis (Fig. S2). And the BET value of PbBiO2Cl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br is determined to be 12.41 and 15.17 m2 g−1. As is well known to all, the higher surface area is beneficial to provide more exposed reactive sites and transport paths for reactants and products, probably resulting in the enhanced photocatalytic performance [30].

The morphology of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br was studied by TEM analysis. In Fig. 1e and f, it can be found that the as-prepared nanocomposite catalyst displays flower-like spheres with a diameter of 1 µm assembled from nanosheets. Additionally, a certain amount of dots are dispersed around the surface of nanosheets. To further observe this microstructure, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurement was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1g, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.321 nm, 0.278 nm and 0.282 nm corresponding to the (002) plane of CQDs, (020) crystallographic plane of PbBiO2Cl and (110) crystallographic plane of PbBiO2Br, respectively [14,27]. And these crystal planes have tight mutual contact, which demonstrate that CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite catalyst have been constructed successfully. In Fig. 1h, the spatial distribution of various elements in CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite are studied. These images indicate that the Pb, Bi, O, Cl and Br elements are distributed in the nanocomposite, and the C element is homogenously distributed throughout the whole nanocomposite, further suggesting that CQDs are dispersed uniformly on the PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres.

The surface chemical states and compositions of the obtained catalysts was investigated by X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra ( Fig. 2). The survey XPS spectrum shows the co-existence of Pb, Bi, O, Cl, Br, C elements in CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite (Fig. 2a). In Fig. 2b, the peaks at roughly 142.4 and 137.5 eV are ascribed to the Pb 4f of Pb2+ [14]. In Fig. 2c, the strong peaks located at 163.8 and 158.5 are assigned to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, manifesting the presence of trivalent bismuth [31]. The binding energy of O 1s centered at 529.2 eV (Fig. 2d) is assigned to the oxygen of PbBiO2X. The XPS peaks of the Cl 2p centered at 198.8 and 197.2 eV are ascribed to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 of Cl- (Fig. 2e) [14]. In addition, the binding energies of Br 3d (Fig. 2f) located at 68.8.1 and 67.8 eV, corresponding to Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2 of Br- [13]. Fig. 2g shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, in which three deconvoluted peaks can be ascribed to C-C (284.1 eV), C-O (285.7 eV) and C-N (287.6 eV), respectively [32]. The XPS results further prove the presence of PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Br and CQDs in the CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite.

The photoreactivity of the as-prepared catalysts was assessed by decomposition of CIP under broadband light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). As we all know, CIP is classified as the second generation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, which has been proved to cause environmental harm and displays toxic effects in the surface water and groundwater [33]. As a consequence, it is crucial to reduce CIP concentrations to reach the national standards. Herein, CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite are tentatively employed to remove CIP, and the importance of CQDs can also be confirmed from another angle. The CIP adsorption capacity over different samples is displayed in Fig. S3. The dark adsorption experiment results demonstrate that the adsorption capacity can be improved thanks to π-π interactions between CQDs and CIP molecule [31]. In Fig. 3a, the degradation efficiency is comparatively low while adding single PbBiO2Cl or PbBiO2Br. The degradation rate of CIP is only 28 % within 120 min while employing PbBiO2Cl. For PbBiO2Br, the degradation rate of CIP is almost negligible. Surprisingly, the removal efficiency is obviously improved after the formation of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure. And CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br show higher photocatalytic efficiency than that of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, highlighting the key role of CQDs in environmental purification. Notably, 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br is obviously superior to other CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br samples. This can be explained that too much CQDs may generate adverse shielding effect, hindering PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br’s surface to absorb visible light photons and overlaying the reactive sites for photocatalysis via CQDs agglomeration [31,34]. Fig. 3b shows the photocatalytic kinetics fit of CIP degradation on account of pseudo-first-order model (Langmuir-Hinshelwood model). It can be clearly seen that the degradation efficiency of CIP can be enhanced obviously after adding CQDs. Specifically, the degradation rate constant of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br is approximately 3.02, 4.64 and 130 times higher than those of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br.

To further highlight the importance of ionic liquids, the photoreactivity of PbBiO2Cl-KCl, PbBiO2Br-KBr, PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr and 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr were carried out. As shown in Fig. S4, the degradation efficiency is almost negligible while employing PbBiO2Cl-KCl, PbBiO2Br-KBr or PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr as a catalyst. And the degradation rate of CIP is only 25.5 % within 120 min while employing 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl-KCl/PbBiO2Br-KBr. This can be interpreted as ionic liquid is employed as template and reaction source to regulate the growth of PbBiO2X crystals [13]. More importantly, ionic liquid can play as dispersant, which is beneficial for the well distribution of CQDs around PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres [24]. To evaluate the catalyst’s reusability, five consecutive cycles have been conducted over 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br photocatalyst. In Fig. S5a, the degradation rate displays no significant variation in the elimination of CIP, manifesting the preferable catalytic stability of the nanocomposite catalyst. Furthermore, XRD patterns further confirm the well-retained structure of the nanocomposite catalyst after photoirradiation reaction (Fig. S5b).

TC is a broad-spectrum antibiotic, which has been widely used in disease treatment and other fields. Nevertheless, residual TC in water and soil can cause great harm to the environment and human health [35]. It is the first time reported by employing CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br for TC degradation, and the degradation plots are shown in Fig. 3c. The degradation rate of TC achieves 23.2 %, 8.8 % and 36.4 % within 120 min while employing PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Br and PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br. Surprisingly, more than 64 % of TC can be eliminated over PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure loading with 5 wt% CQDs. And the calculating rate constant is 0.0094 min−1, much better than those of PbBiO2Cl (0.0024 min−1), PbBiO2Br (0.0006 min−1) and PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br (0.0041 min−1) (Fig. 3d). The above experimental data demonstrate the key roles of CQDs during a photocatalytic reaction process [36,37].

The optical characteristics of the obtained catalysts across the UV–vis region are recorded by diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). In Fig. 4a, pristine PbBiO2Cl displays intrinsic bandgap absorption from 200 nm to 508 nm. For PbBiO2Br, the absorption wavelength ranges from 200 nm to 525 nm. This can be attributed to their intrinsic band-to-band transition [31]. Based on the plots of (αhν)
0.5 versus hν, the band gap (E g) of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br is determined to be 2.31 and 2.06 eV (Fig. 4b). After the formation of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure, the absorption wavelength range shows no significant change. The enhanced photocatalytic performance can be assigned to the effective separation of photo-induced carriers. What is more, the optical absorption is substantially extended to the long wavelength region (>500 nm) after the decoration of CQDs. As a result, it may boost the generation of photo-induced carriers thanks to the enhanced light-harvesting capability. Consequently, more reactive species can be involved into photocatalytic reaction.

To clarify the charge separation and transfer kinetics over the obtained catalysts, photoluminescence (PL) and photoelectrochemical measurements are performed. Fig. 4c displays that all the catalysts possess an obvious emission peak centered at 440 nm. More importantly, 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br shows an apparent quenching in comparison to PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Br and PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br. This characterization result indicates an improving separation probability of photogenerated carriers, which is beneficial for visible light-driven catalytic reactions [13]. The charge separation/transfer process is further monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent measurements. In Fig. 4d, the semicircle arc of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br in the Nyquist plot is the smallest among the four catalysts, reflecting the lowest interfacial charge transfer resistence [38–40]. Additionally, the arc radius of PbBiO2Br displays the highest, which is consistent with photocatalytic activities. The results of EIS test are further confirmed by transient photocurrent. Fig. 4e exhibits the transient photocurrent responses of the four catalysts under chopped light irradiation. It can be found that the photocurrent density is in the order PbBiO2Br< PbBiO2Cl< PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br< 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br, which coincide with the trend of photocatalytic performance. The photocurrent results demonstrate that the construction of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure is conducive to the separation of photoinduced charge carriers, and the charge separation efficiency of CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br is superior to that of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br [41]. Taking the above PL and photoelectrochemical results into account, CQDs modification and PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure will boost charge migration and separation, which is favorable for the generation of reactive species.

Apart from the light absorption efficiency and spatial separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers, the energy band structure also plays a critical role in determining photocatalytic efficiency. The total density of states of valence band (VB) can be obtained based on the valence-band XPS spectra with the Fermi level (E f) of semiconductors is 0 eV (Fig. 4f). The VB value of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br are measured to be 1.80 and 1.69 eV. And the positive slopes of Mott-Schottky plots show that both PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br can be classified into n-type semiconductor. According to the extrapolation of X intercept in the Mott-Schottky plots, the flat band potential of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br are measured to be − 0.55 and − 0.75 V vs NHE (pH = 7), respectively. With regarding to the n-type semiconductors, the Fermi level is close to the flat band potential [42]. As a result, the VB value of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br are 1.25 and 0.94 V vs NHE, respectively. On the basis of the E g, the CB minimum of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br occur at about − 1.06 and − 1.12 V vs NHE, respectively.

ESR (electron spin resonance) technique and free radicals capturing tests are conducted to ascertain the major active species involved into the degradation process [43,44]. The ESR analysis are presented in Fig. 5a, b. In the darkness, no characteristic signals can be observed for DMPO-O2 •- and DMPO-•OH from the two catalysts, and no reactive species can be trapped. Nevertheless, typical characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 •- can be detected under photoirradiation. And the DMPO-O2 •- signals of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br are obviously higher than that of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br. Furthermore, DMPO-•OH can be trapped from the two catalysts upon light illumination. And similar strong signal peaks can be obtained for 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br. ESR analysis demonstrate that both superoxide radical (O2 •-) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) can be generated upon light illumination. And the modification of CQDs is beneficial for the generation of reactive oxygen species. To further verify the presence of these active species, free radical quenching tests are carried out in the presence of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br (Fig. 5c). After the addition of IPA, AO and TEA, notable inhibition of photocatalytic activity can be observed, indicating that •OH and direct hole oxidation play as critical roles during visible light-driven catalytic reactions. More than that, after N2 is pumped into the reaction solution, the degradation efficiency declines greatly [45]. This can be explained that a large amount of O2 •- is generated and O2 •- act as a key role in the degradation process. The analysis of radical capture tests are in accord with ESR results.

Considering that the above experimental results, the separation and transformation paths of photo-created carriers involved into visible light-driven degradation of antibiotics are presented in Fig. 5d. Under broad light irradiation, electrons in the VB of PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br can be excited and then migrated to the CB. Thanks to the matched structure and tight interface contact, electrons in the CB of PbBiO2Br can transfer to the CB of PbBiO2Cl, while holes in the VB of PbBiO2Cl can move to the VB of PbBiO2Br. This can result in the spatial separation of holes and electrons in PbBiO2Cl and PbBiO2Br. After the modification of CQDs, the mobility of photo-created electrons can be further enhanced thanks to the electron acceptor property of CQDs. Consequently, more powerful oxidants participate in photocatalytic reactions. Even so, the holes on the VB can not thermodynamically oxidize H2O (H2O/•OH 2.34 V vs. NHE) or OH- (•OH/OH- 1.99 V vs. NHE) to generate •OH [46–48]. It can be speculated that the signal peaks of DMPO-•OH do not derive from the oxidation of holes. Considering that more O2 •- can be generated over CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br via the reduction of oxygen molecules, a portion of O2 •- are directly involved into the degradation of antibiotics, and excessive O2 •- can react with H+ to yield •OH (2 O2 •- + 2 H+ → 2•OH + O2) [32]. Therefore, the main reactive species including O2 •-, •OH and holes generated under visible light illumination engage in antibiotics elimination together, synergistically boosting the enhancement of photocatalytic performance over CQDs/PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br nanocomposite.

In conclusion, CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres have been fabricated via a simple ionothermal method. In this prepared procedure, ionic liquid serve as high performance template, reactant and dispersant, which is beneficial for the uniform distribution of CQDs around PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br microspheres. And the photocatalytic activity for removing antibiotics is greatly enhanced. Construction of PbBiO2Cl/PbBiO2Br heterostructure and CQDs modification can successfully reduce charge carriers recombination and accelerate the transformation of photoinduced hole-electron pairs to the catalyst surface. Therefore, more reactive oxygen species can be generated and involved into the degradation of antibiotics. This work may offer some insights about constructing carbon-based materials modified heterostructure catalyst, which will be widely welcomed in environmental purification and energy conversion.

Xifang Ruan: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Aiyun Sun: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ting Zhou: Data curation, Investigation. Haijie Zhang: Data curation, Investigation. Meng Ye: Data curation, Investigation. Xingwang Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jianjian Yi: Data curation, Investigation. Qingsong Hu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Binxian Gu: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

PCRRF21036) and the Science and Technology Innovation Fund Project of Yangzhou University Students (NO. X20220558).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, G.P. Liu, X.Y. Xu, B. Wang, S.X. Sun, J.X. Xia, H.M. Li, Oxygen vacancies mediated Bi12O17Cl2 ultrathin nanobelts: boosting molecular oxygen activation for efficient organic pollutants degradation, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 609 (2022) 23–32.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mesh-like BiOBr/Bi2S3 nanoarray heterojunction with hierarchical pores and oxygen vacancies for broadband CO2 photoreduction
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Xi, W.H. Mo, Z.X. Fan, L.X. Hu, W.B. Chen, Y. Zhang, P. Wang, S.X. Zhong, Y.L. Zhao, S. Bai, A mesh-like BiOBr/Bi2S3 nanoarray heterojunction with hierarchical pores and oxygen vacancies for broadband CO2 photoreduction, J. Mater. Chem. A 10 (2022) 20934.
